title: Oscar gates drugs on prescriber specialty; Florida Blue usually doesn't
summary: For specialty drugs, Oscar routinely requires the prescriber to be (or consult) a relevant specialist. Florida Blue's equivalent policies generally omit a specialist requirement, leaning on indication tables and FDA labeling.
detail: Across matched drug policies, Oscar's first criterion is almost always “prescribed by or in consultation with” a named specialty — e.g. dermatologist/allergist for **Dupixent** (Oscar PG026 vs FL 09-J2000-80), ophthalmologist or retinal specialist for **Syfovre** (PG150 vs 09-J4000-47), infectious-disease/heme/transplant specialist for **Livtencity** (PG113 vs 09-J4000-12), and hematologist-oncologist for **Yescarta** (CG063 vs 09-J2000-95). Florida Blue's matching guidelines typically do not state a prescriber-specialty requirement. Net effect: Oscar applies a tighter prescriber gate on the same therapies.

title: Oscar sets explicit age/weight minimums; Florida Blue defers to FDA labeling
summary: Oscar's drug criteria commonly hard-code a minimum age (and sometimes weight). Florida Blue more often ties age to “within FDA labeling,” leaving it indication-dependent.
detail: Oscar states fixed thresholds — e.g. **≥12 years** for **Cibinqo** (PG111) and **Livtencity** (PG113, plus **≥35 kg**), **≥18 years** for **Syfovre** (PG150) and **Izervay** (PG168), **≥5 years** for **Benlysta** (PG014). Florida Blue's parallel policies (e.g. abrocitinib 09-J4000-27, dupilumab 09-J2000-80) instead require the member's age to be “within FDA labeling for the requested indication,” or allow prescriber justification. Reviewers comparing eligibility for a specific patient should expect age to be a frequent point of divergence (53 of 120 matched topics show an age-criterion difference in the automated scan).

title: Step therapy is codified differently
summary: Oscar frequently requires a documented trial-and-failure of specific prior therapies; Florida Blue's structure centers on continuation-of-therapy and discloses Florida's step-therapy exception law.
detail: For **Cibinqo** (atopic dermatitis), Oscar PG111 requires the member to have “adequately tried and failed TWO of” named therapies for ≥ 8 weeks each in the past year; Florida Blue 09-J4000-27 instead builds its first criterion around recent prior use of the drug itself and indication-specific tables. Florida Blue policies also reference **Florida law 627.42393**, under which members/providers can request a step-therapy exception — a state-specific process Oscar's guidelines don't mention. 38 of 120 matched topics show a step-therapy difference in the automated scan.

title: Florida Blue restricts site of care toward self-administration
summary: Several Florida Blue drug policies declare that provider-administered (office/outpatient) administration is “not medically necessary” for self-injectable products — a site-of-care limit Oscar's matching policies don't emphasize.
detail: Florida Blue 09-J1000-35 states that administration of subcutaneous **belimumab (Benlysta)** and **voclosporin (Lupkynis)** “in a provider-administered setting such as an outpatient hospital, ambulatory surgical suite, physician office, or emergency facility is not considered medically necessary.” This pushes members to self-administration/specialty pharmacy. Oscar's Benlysta guideline (PG014) focuses on clinical eligibility without the equivalent site-of-care exclusion.

title: Opposite consolidation strategies — and Oscar delegates some domains
summary: Florida Blue consolidates drug classes into combined guidelines; Oscar keeps one guideline per drug but bundles some medical/biologic classes — and routes domains like advanced imaging to eviCore.
detail: Florida Blue folds many individual drugs into class guidelines — e.g. **Multiple Sclerosis: Oral and Self Injectable Therapy (09-J5000-44)** covers Mavenclad, Ponvory, Gilenya, Aubagio, Ofatumumab and Mayzent; CAR-T agents collapse into 09-J3000-94. Oscar keeps these as separate per-drug guidelines (PG227, PG230, …). Conversely, Oscar publishes class-level **medical** guidelines (e.g. *Biologics for Chronic Respiratory* CG100, *HER2-Targeted Agents* CG101) where Florida Blue has per-drug policies, and Oscar's clinical-guidelines page **delegates advanced imaging/radiology to eviCore**. This is the single biggest reason the catalogs look different (754 vs 387 guidelines) and why many “gaps” are organizational, not coverage gaps.

title: SMA gene therapy (Zolgensma): different eligibility ceilings
summary: Both require genetic confirmation, but Oscar caps eligibility at age <2 and disease onset before 6 months, while Florida Blue instead leans on SMN2 copy number, anti-AAV9 antibody titer and ventilation status.
detail: Oscar CG061 requires age **< 2 years** at treatment, symptom **onset before 6 months**, a neurologist/neuromuscular prescriber, and **1–3 SMN2 copies**. Florida Blue 09-J3000-30 requires **≤ 4 SMN2 copies**, a pre-treatment **anti-AAV9 titer ≤ 1:50**, liver assessment/monitoring, and that the member is **not invasive-ventilation dependent** — without the explicit age-<2/onset-<6-months ceiling. The same therapy, two materially different eligibility envelopes.

title: Bariatric surgery: Oscar maintains a dedicated adolescent policy
summary: Oscar splits bariatric surgery into adult (CG008) and adolescent (CG009) guidelines with pediatric BMI-percentile criteria; Florida Blue addresses it within a single policy.
detail: Oscar CG009 sets adolescent-specific criteria — age **13–17 with physical maturity (≥95% predicted adult stature by bone age)**, **BMI ≥40 (or ≥140% of the 95th percentile)**, or **BMI ≥35 with a qualifying comorbidity** (T2DM, moderate-severe OSA, pseudotumor cerebri, severe NASH). Florida Blue 02-40000-10 handles bariatric surgery (including revision and balloon devices, several marked investigational) without a separate adolescent track in the matched policy.

title: Strong convergence on objective-threshold topics
summary: Where coverage hinges on objective lab/imaging thresholds, the two payers largely agree — home oxygen and major-joint arthroplasty are near-identical.
detail: **Home oxygen** — both adopt the classic thresholds: PaO2 ≤ 55 mmHg / SpO2 ≤ 88%, or 56–59 mmHg / 89% with cor pulmonale, edema or polycythemia (FL 09-E0400-00 vs Oscar CG005). **Hip arthroplasty** — both require advanced joint destruction (**Kellgren-Lawrence grade III/IV**) plus persistent pain and ≥ 3 months of failed conservative care (FL 02-20000-65 vs Oscar CG070). On these, a denial under one payer would likely be a denial under the other.

title: Catalog scope: 120 overlapping topics, hundreds unique — mostly structural
summary: Florida Blue publishes far more individual guidelines (754 vs 387). Of these, 120 topics match across payers; 624 are Florida-Blue-only and 185 Oscar-only — but most “only” topics reflect granularity, class-bundling or vendor delegation, not proven non-coverage.
detail: Florida-Blue-only topics are dominated by **Pharmacy**, **Surgery** and **Radiology** — areas where FL writes per-drug and per-imaging-study guidelines that Oscar either bundles or delegates (Oscar routes advanced imaging to eviCore). Oscar-only topics include dedicated guidelines for areas like **Hearing Aids & Implants (CG001)**, **Home Births & Birth Centers (CG038)**, **Erectile Dysfunction (CG037)**, **Acupuncture (CG013)** and **Enteral/Oral Nutrition (CG011)**. Treat these lists as “who publishes a dedicated guideline,” not a definitive coverage yes/no — absence of a standalone guideline can mean coverage is handled under a broader policy, a delegated vendor, or plan documents.
